selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"As a man who’s spent decades shaping wood into something solid and real, I’ve learned that truth is often found in the grain—something you can feel, not just see. Social media, in contrast, feels like a world of polished veneer, where everything looks perfect but can be hollow. When I see fake content—like those outrageous claims about miracle cures or shocking political lies—I don’t just scroll past; I pause. I remember my father, a PhD in physics, used to say, 'If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.' That’s my first rule. I don’t trust what I don’t understand, and I don’t trust what comes from a source I can’t verify. I’ve seen friends fall for scams because they believed a post that looked official, like a government alert. I’ve seen my daughter’s school chat flooded with misinformation about vaccines—fear spread faster than facts. That’s why I now check the origin: who posted it? Is it a known, reputable outlet? Or just a page with no history? I use the few tools I know—checking the URL, looking up the author, even searching the claim on trusted sites like the Italian Ministry of Health or fact-checking groups like FactCheck.org. I also talk to my family. I tell them, 'Don’t react in anger—think like a carpenter. Measure twice, cut once.' When I see something that stirs me, I wait. I don’t share it until I’ve checked. It’s not about being cynical; it’s about being responsible. I care about my community, and spreading lies is like building a house with rotten wood—it might stand for a while, but it’ll collapse. I may not be tech-savvy, but I’ve got common sense, and I’ve learned that the most powerful tool on social media isn’t a like or a share—it’s patience and a good eye. I don’t need to be a digital expert to know what’s real. I just need to remember that truth, like a well-made table, is built slowly, with care, and tested by time.","reasoning_summary":"The user, a 56-year-old Italian carpenter with low openness, low conscientiousness, high extraversion, low agreeableness, and high neuroticism, draws on personal experience, family values, and practical wisdom. His vocational background (carpentry) informs his metaphor of truth as something solid and built with care. He references his father’s academic background as a source of rational skepticism. His high neuroticism explains his sensitivity to misinformation and emotional reactions, which he manages through deliberate action. His daily social media use (one hour) is brief but intentional. He relies on basic verification techniques—checking sources, URLs, and reputable fact-checkers—consistent with limited digital literacy. He emphasizes community responsibility and emotional regulation, aligning with his high extraversion (social engagement) and Christian values (truth, integrity). His opinion reflects a grounded, pragmatic, and cautious approach to misinformation, rooted in lived experience rather than technical expertise.","tone":"pragmatic, reflective, slightly cautious with a touch of moral urgency","citations":["FactCheck.org","Italian Ministry of Health"],"safety_notes":"none","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean","opinion"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a man who’s spent decades shaping wood into something solid and real, I’ve learned that truth is often found in the grain—something you can feel, not just see. Social media, in contrast, feels like a world of polished veneer, where everything looks perfect but can be hollow. When I see fake content—like those outrageous claims about miracle cures or shocking political lies—I don’t just scroll past; I pause. I remember my father, a PhD in physics, used to say, 'If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.' That’s my first rule. I don’t trust what I don’t understand, and I don’t trust what comes from a source I can’t verify. I’ve seen friends fall for scams because they believed a post that looked official, like a government alert. I’ve seen my daughter’s school chat flooded with misinformation about vaccines—fear spread faster than facts. That’s why I now check the origin: who posted it? Is it a known, reputable outlet? Or just a page with no history? I use the few tools I know—checking the URL, looking up the author, even searching the claim on trusted sites like the Italian Ministry of Health or fact-checking groups like FactCheck.org. I also talk to my family. I tell them, 'Don’t react in anger—think like a carpenter. Measure twice, cut once.' When I see something that stirs me, I wait. I don’t share it until I’ve checked. It’s not about being cynical; it’s about being responsible. I care about my community, and spreading lies is like building a house with rotten wood—it might stand for a while, but it’ll collapse. I may not be tech-savvy, but I’ve got common sense, and I’ve learned that the most powerful tool on social media isn’t a like or a share—it’s patience and a good eye. I don’t need to be a digital expert to know what’s real. I just need to remember that truth, like a well-made table, is built slowly, with care, and tested by time.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The user, a 56-year-old Italian carpenter with low openness, low conscientiousness, high extraversion, low agreeableness, and high neuroticism, draws on personal experience, family values, and practical wisdom. His vocational background (carpentry) informs his metaphor of truth as something solid and built with care. He references his father’s academic background as a source of rational skepticism. His high neuroticism explains his sensitivity to misinformation and emotional reactions, which he manages through deliberate action. His daily social media use (one hour) is brief but intentional. He relies on basic verification techniques—checking sources, URLs, and reputable fact-checkers—consistent with limited digital literacy. He emphasizes community responsibility and emotional regulation, aligning with his high extraversion (social engagement) and Christian values (truth, integrity). His opinion reflects a grounded, pragmatic, and cautious approach to misinformation, rooted in lived experience rather than technical expertise.
response_parsed.tone: pragmatic, reflective, slightly cautious with a touch of moral urgency
